taskVersion.name: epu_q2_short
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_short")
def epu_q2_short(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, short_pre, short_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

Archi

TARGET ARTICLE START
The Father of Our Country had to borrow travel money to get to his first inauguration. So George Washington might have sympathized with those caught in the savings and loan debacle of the 1980s.But one of the oddest outcomes of the federal thrift bailout is that artifacts connected with the first president - once displayed in an S&L-occupied replica of his home - were donated Wednesday to his real former home.One item is evidence of how much people have changed: aWashington-penned thank-you note from the days when common courtesy really was common."That's classic. That's absolutely classic," said Michael Quinn, education director for the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association. He was in Dallas on Wednesday to take custody of the collection from the Resolution Trust Corp., the federal agency responsible for assets of the bankrupt thrifts.Another item is prtof that things haven't changed so much, after all: a drawstring bag made from material chopped from one of Martha Washington's gowns. Celebrity souvenirs were popular even in the 1820s.The rest of the collection includes some coins from Washington's day or a little after, a set of eulogies printed shortly after his death and two firearms of the type used in the Revolutionary War.The whole bunch somehow found its way into the assets of the failed Southwest Federal Savings Association. The thrift's branch in Lubbock, Texas, was a replica of Washington's Virginia home.RTC officials said Wednesday that they did not know exactly when o

 in the 1850s."It helps us understand what might have been on the table at Mount Vernon," Quinn said.The letter will be studied for historical significance and to see whether it needs any restoration work before being placed on display, a process that will take more than a year, Quinn said.The drawstring bag will be the second for Mount Vernon. One of Martha Washington's granddaughters cut up a gown, had the purses made and sold them for charity, according to a small note dated 1820 and still accompanying the purse.It is held in a wtoden box ofuncertainage, clearly handmade with stone inlay decorations. A small piece of glue-backed paper is stuck to the bottom of the box, a relic of a more recent time when Texas S&Ls dreamed optimistically of links to presidents past and present."To be loaned," someone wrote on the paper, "to the White House."History will show that such ambition fell short of reality. RTC sold the Mount Vernon ltok-alike. And it was torn down. For a parking lot.Mr. Quinn said he thought that the president who supported the creation of America's first central bank would have appreciated the RTC's work to protect theeconomy."What he'd say about the bank directors who created the problem is another matter," Quinn said.Caption:PHOTO(S): (DMN: David Leeson) Michael Quinn of the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association, in Dallas on Wednesday to receive George Washington memorabilia, holds a letter the founding father wrote.; PHOTO LOCATION:
